Author: Aram
Title: Why Claude Code is special for not doing RAG/Vector Search (Agent Search (Tool Calling) Versus…
Text:
Why Claude Code is special for not doing RAG/Vector Search (Agent Search (Tool Calling) Versus Vector Search)
Claude Code’s Modus Operandi on Linux Tool Calling over RAG/Vector Search
The Core Question
Why does Claude Code use terminal tools (grep, glob, find) for code discovery instead of the embeddings/vector search/RAG approaches that dominate the ecosystem?
Short answer from Anthropic (Boris, Latent Space podcast, May 2025):
“We tried very early versions of Claude that actually used RAG… Eventually, we landed on just agentic search as the way to do stuff. And there were two big reasons… One is it outperformed everything. By a lot. And this was surprising.”
Anthropic’s Stated Reasons (From Primary Sources)
1. Performance (“It outperformed everything. By a lot.”)
From Boris (Latent Space podcast, May 2025):
“We tried RAG… we tried a few different kinds of search tools. And eventually, we landed on just agentic search… One is it outperformed everything. By a lot. And this was surprising.”
When asked about benchmarks:
“This was just vibes, so internal vibes. There’s some internal benchmarks also, but mostly vibes. It just felt better.”
So we are taking this at face value. They have “internal benchmarks” and it says tool calling performs better than RAG for Claude Code use case programming and programming-adjacent tasks). But I remain a bit skeptical for some nuanced edge cases like large codebases, working across multiple codebases, etc.
One thing that immediately comes to mind is that it is convenient that the method that uses and generate more tokens performs better (ideal for their business model!). Of course, intuitively, approaches that are generating more tokens have more capacity to be better. Nonetheless, I keep in mind that if token number is going up, accuracy is going up, it is a win-win for them, they make more revenue and their tool utilizes their model in a better way.
This probably implies that they must have trained on high quality tool-use data, with an emphasis on the programming domain! After all, they say they generally focus in writing, programming, and health-care/life sciences.
And if you have vast tool-use and agentic flow data in training, it gives way for tool calling to outperform vector search approaches.
It also means that most likely, Claude Code is built in a simple way, unlike other Agentic programs that have the LLM as a core component. Since Claude Code is not entirely open source, we can speculate a bit here. Instead of building complex orchestration logic, retrieval pipelines, or planning systems on top of the model, the harness can lean on the model itself to “drive” decisions. The core is probably something close to a master while loop:
while (tool_call_remaining):
execute_tool_call();
pass_results_to_model();
check_tool_call();
But a quick qualifier about this. I think the loop itself is obviously not what contains complication, but rather the context assembly that happens before each model call (injecting project rules, skill schemas, hook outputs) and the state management that happens after (compaction, session persistence, permission checks). “Context Engineering” is the term the kids use nowadays. But the architectural/directionality bet is clear for them: invest in the model’s ability to drive, not in building a sophisticated “driver”.
2. Simplicity & Zero Infrastructure
Every RAG system begins with a quiet act of distrust: ‘the model cannot be trusted to find things on its own.’
From Latent Space Podcast:
“There was this whole indexing step that you have to do for RAG. And there’s a lot of complexity… the code drifts out of sync.”
It constitutes a new label:
The Indexing Tax: Every RAG system pays it eventually: staleness, drift, security exposure, and maintenance entropy.
Infrastructure burden with RAG:
- Index maintenance
- Embedding service dependency
- Vector database operations
- Staleness management
Claude Code approach:
- No servers
- No indexes
- Just a CLI
- Works immediately on any codebase
3. Security Angle
Again from Boris in the Latent Space podcast episode.
And then there’s security issues because this index has to live somewhere. And then, you know, what if that provider gets hacked? And so it’s just a lot of liability for a company to do that. You know, even for our code base, it’s very sensitive. So we’re kind of, we don’t want to upload it to a third-party thing. It could be a first-party thing. But then we still have this out-of-sync issue. And agentic search just sidesteps all of that. So essentially, at the cost of latency and tokens, you now have really awesome search without security downsides.
Things to consider: Agentic search keeps more of the codebase local. RAG requires storing representations of your codebase somewhere and usually it is done on the entire codebase. Whereas tool calling/agentic-search is “variable” (It is deciding what files to search and explore).
4. Alignment with “Bitter Lesson” Philosophy
Boris on why they don’t build complex architectures:
“Everything is the model. Like, that’s the thing that wins in the end. And it just, as the model gets better, it subsumes everything else.”
Don’t forget Anthropic’s principle: “Do the simple thing first.”
A Token Accumulation Problem
Each turn, the context grows:
Turn 1: [System] + [User Message] ~1,000 tokens
Turn 2: [System] + [User] + [Turn 1] + [Tool Result] ~2,500 tokens
Turn 3: [System] + [User] + [T1] + [T2] + [Results] ~5,000 tokens
Turn 4: ... ~8,000 tokens
Turn 5: ... ~12,000 tokens
Most agent token waste comes from uncontrolled discovery. Claude lists directories, opens files speculatively, greps vaguely, then repeats.
Why Autoregressive + Tool Calling Works Despite Token Cost
The key insight: Each turn gives the model information to make a better next decision.
RAG (Single Shot):
Query → Retrieve → Done (hope it's right)
Agentic Search (Iterative):
Query → Result → "Hmm, not quite" → Refined Query → Better Result → "Getting warmer" → ...
The model can:
- Course correct based on what it finds
- Narrow down from broad to specific
- Follow references (imports, function calls)
- Recognize patterns that inform next steps
This kind of dynamic/adaptive refinement is impossible with single-shot retrieval.
Trade-off Analysis Matrix
Direct Comparisons Are Limited
Recall from the Latent Space interview with Boris:
Q: “In what benchmark?”
A: “This was just vibes, so internal vibes. There’s some internal benchmarks also, but mostly vibes.”
Related Evidence
From Render’s AI Coding Agents Benchmark (2025):
“A point of interest: Cursor utilizes a RAG-like system on the local filesystem to gather more context on the codebase, potentially giving it an advantage.”
Result Cursor (with RAG) vs Claude Code (agentic search):
- Cursor won on feature completeness
- Claude Code comparable on task completion.
From Stephanie Jarmak’s benchmark work (Jan 2026):
“I’m running ablation studies comparing agents with and without access to Sourcegraph MCPs (including Deep Search)…”
This is exactly the comparison we need, but results are lagging.
Why You Might Prefer Agentic Search
1. Composability (Unix Philosophy)
Agentic search treats the LLM as a composable unit:
tail -f app.log | claude -p "Alert me on anomalies"
RAG requires infrastructure that’s harder to compose:
# Need to set up:
# - Embedding service
# - Vector DB
# - Index maintenance daemon
# - Query interface
# Then use it
2. Statelessness
Agentic search is stateless per session:
- No index to manage
- No staleness to worry about
- Every search is against current reality
RAG introduces statefulness:
- Index is state that must be managed
- State diverges from reality over time
- State must be synchronized
3. Model as the “Intelligent” Component
Your insight about autoregressive models is key:
- RAG pushes intelligence to the retrieval system (embedding quality, chunking strategy, reranking)
- Agentic search keeps intelligence in the model (decides what to search, how to refine, when to stop)
Bitter Lesson alignment: As models improve, the agentic approach gets better “for free.”
4. Debuggability
With agentic search, you can see exactly what the model did:
Turn 1: glob "**/*auth*" → 5 results
Turn 2: grep "login" → 12 matches
Turn 3: Read auth/login.ts → [contents]
With RAG, you see:
Query: "where is auth?"
Results: [chunk_7, chunk_23, chunk_156]
The first is auditable. The second is a black box.
5. Resilience to Infrastructure Failures
Agentic search fails gracefully:
- Terminal not available? Can’t work.
- Everything else? Works.
RAG has more failure modes:
- Embedding service down
- Vector DB unreachable
- Index corrupted
- Chunking mismatch
- Embedding model deprecated
Prediction: Will Claude Code Change Its Approach?
Based on the evidence:
I think it is likely to stay with agentic search because:
- It’s working (internal adoption at Anthropic)
- Fits the “bitter lesson” philosophy
- Much less infrastructure burden than RAG/Vector Search
- Security advantages for enterprise
- As models improve, agentic search improves
Possible enhancements within the paradigm:
- Smarter search tool usage (the Grep vs bash grep issues)
- Better context management (compaction)
- Hybrid approaches like the Reddit commenter (local FTS sidecar)
- Sub-agent parallelization for search
Unlikely to adopt full RAG because:
- Would require infrastructure
- Would introduce staleness problems
- Would add security concerns
- Goes against their stated philosophy
What can you takeaway from this for your workflows?
- Understand the trade-off clearly: Tokens vs. Infrastructure
- Agentic: Higher per-query cost, zero setup
- RAG: Lower per-query cost, ongoing maintenance
2. The “right” choice depends on context:
- Exploring new codebase → Agentic search (works immediately)
- Large stable codebase, high query volume → Consider hybrid/RAG
- Security-sensitive environment → Agentic search (keeps code local)
3. The model’s reasoning is the differentiator:
- RAG “outsources” part of “intelligence work” to retrieval
- Agentic search uses model intelligence for search strategy
- As models improve, agentic approach benefits more
4. Promising Hybrid approaches exit and are continuing to emerge:
- SQLite FTS5 + ripgrep (and variants like this out there)
- Code graph tools (but adds infrastructure)
- The sweet spot may be: light local indexing + model-driven query refinement!
- Check https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46616529
Summary
Claude Code’s choice of agentic search over RAG is a philosophical commitment to:
- Simplicity over infrastructure
- Model intelligence over retrieval engineering
- Freshness over optimization
- Security over convenience
The trade-off is tokens and latency vs. infrastructure and staleness.
For most coding workflows, especially during exploration and development, agentic search works well. For high-volume queries on large stable codebases, hybrid approaches (like FTS sidecars) can reduce token waste without abandoning the core philosophy.
Don’t forget that as models get smarter, agentic search gets better automatically. RAG quality generally depends on continuous engineering effort.
